1:
"And Joshua gathered all the tribes of Israel to Shechem, and called for the elders of Israel, and for their heads, and for their judges, and for their officers; and they presented themselves before God." The very brief verse notes Joshua gathering all Israel to Shechem before God. The grammatical voices note the assembly. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Joshua gathered all the tribes of Israel to Shechem, and called for the elders... and they presented themselves before God" — Joshua assembled all Israel at Shechem, the place of the patriarchs, before God; the and Joshua gathered all the tribes of Israel to Shechem (the gathering at Shechem); the and called for the elders of Israel, and for their heads, and for their judges, and for their officers (the leaders called); the and they presented themselves before God (before God); the figure (the solemn assembly at Shechem, the place of Abraham's altar and Jacob's covenant, before God; the truth that Joshua gathered the people to the place of the patriarchs to renew the covenant, Genesis 12:6; the figure of the people brought before God to hear his word and renew the covenant; the assembly at Shechem; the covenant renewal, vv.2). The verse holds together the assembly before God.

On "And Joshua gathered all the tribes of Israel to Shechem, and called for the elders of Israel, and for their heads, and for their judges, and for their officers," the witnesses note the gathering. Gill notes Joshua gathering all Israel to Shechem (the assembly, Genesis 12:6) — the gathering (to Shechem).

On "and they presented themselves before God," the grammatical-historical Ellicott and grammatical-historical note the presence: "they presented themselves before God" — the people before God to renew the covenant (the solemn presence); the figure (the people brought before God to hear his word); the providence (the assembly, the rehearsal of God's mercies to follow, vv.2). The verse displays Joshua gathering all Israel to Shechem before God.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the assembly — "Joshua gathered all the tribes of Israel to Shechem... and they presented themselves before God," the assembly (Keil, Gill); "And Joshua gathered all the tribes of Israel to Shechem, and called for the elders of Israel, and for their heads, and for their judges, and for their officers," the gathering (Genesis 12:6, Gill); "and they presented themselves before God," the presence (Ellicott, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the assembly. The verse marks Israel: gathered. The verse displays Shechem: the place. The verse displays the leaders: called. The verse displays God: before whom. The assembly displays the people brought before God to renew the covenant. The verse displays Shechem: of the patriarchs. The verse displays the people: presented. The verse displays the covenant: to be renewed. Joshua gathering all Israel to Shechem before God displays the people brought to renew the covenant: Joshua gathered all the tribes of Israel to Shechem... and they presented themselves before God (Genesis 12:6) — Joshua assembling all Israel at Shechem, the place of Abraham's altar and Jacob's covenant, before God to renew the covenant. The verse displays Joshua rehearsing God's mercies from Abraham onward (vv.2-13).
2:
"And Joshua said unto all the people, Thus saith the LORD God of Israel, Your fathers dwelt on the other side of the flood in old time, even Terah, the father of Abraham, and the father of Nachor: and they served other gods." The witnesses note Joshua's rehearsal that the fathers, even Terah and his house, served other gods beyond the river — the crux of the free electing grace of God, who called Abraham out of idolatry by no merit of his own. On "your fathers dwelt on the other side of the flood... even Terah, the father of Abraham... and they served other gods," interpreters expound the origin. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the free grace of election — "and Joshua said unto all the people, Thus saith the LORD God of Israel, Your fathers dwelt on the other side of the flood in old time, even Terah, the father of Abraham, and the father of Nachor: and they served other gods": Joshua, speaking God's word, declares that Abraham's own father and house were idolaters; the witnesses note the significance — the free and sovereign grace of God, who called Abraham not because he or his fathers were worshippers of the true God, but out of idolatry itself, the line of promise drawn from a house that served other gods (Terah and his family idolaters in Ur and beyond the Euphrates, Abraham called by pure grace, "I took your father Abraham from the other side of the flood," Joshua 24:3; Genesis 11:31); the lesson — that God's election rests on his own free mercy and not on any worthiness in those he chooses, the people reminded that their very origin was idolatry, lest they boast in themselves, "not by works of righteousness which we have done, but according to his mercy he saved us," Titus 3:5; the truth that grace finds men in their sin and calls them out, the root of Israel an idolater rescued by God, "such were some of you: but ye are washed," 1 Corinthians 6:11; the figure — the calling of the sinner out of darkness by sovereign grace, Abraham a pattern of the elect drawn from idolatry; the truth that all boasting is excluded, the people owing all to God's free choice; the figure of the free electing grace of God, who calls his own out of idolatry by no merit of theirs; the call to humility and gratitude in those chosen by grace; the warning against boasting in any worthiness of our own. The figure of the free electing grace of God, who called Abraham out of idolatry.

On "and Joshua said unto all the people, Thus saith the LORD God of Israel, Your fathers dwelt on the other side of the flood in old time, even Terah, the father of Abraham, and the father of Nachor," the grammatical-historical Keil and witnesses note the fathers beyond the river. Keil and Ellicott note Joshua rehearsing that the fathers, Terah and his house, dwelt beyond the Euphrates in old time, before God called Abraham (the origin beyond the river, Genesis 11:31) — the fathers (beyond the flood).

On "and they served other gods," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the idolatry of the fathers. Calvin and Gill note that the fathers, even Abraham's house, served other gods, the line of promise drawn from idolatry by God's free grace, election resting on mercy and not worthiness (the free grace of election, Titus 3:5; 1 Corinthians 6:11) — the figure (the calling of the sinner out of darkness by sovereign grace); the providence (the origin, God's taking and leading of Abraham to follow, vv.3). The verse displays the fathers serving other gods beyond the river.

The witnesses agree the substance: the fathers, even Terah and Abraham's house, served other gods beyond the river, and God called Abraham by free grace (Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of electing grace: your fathers served other gods — the free and sovereign grace of God, who called Abraham not for any worthiness but out of idolatry itself, the line of promise drawn from a house of idolaters (Genesis 11:31), God's election resting on his own free mercy and not on merit (Titus 3:5), grace finding men in their sin and calling them out (1 Corinthians 6:11), all boasting excluded. The verse displays the electing-grace crux. The verse marks the fathers: idolaters. The verse displays Abraham: called from idolatry. The verse displays the grace: free. The verse displays the boasting: excluded. The origin displays the free electing grace of God who calls his own out of idolatry. The verse displays the election: of mercy. The verse displays the sinner: called out. The verse displays the gratitude: due. The fathers serving other gods beyond the river display the crux: your fathers dwelt on the other side of the flood in old time, even Terah, the father of Abraham... and they served other gods (Genesis 11:31; Titus 3:5; 1 Corinthians 6:11) — the free electing grace of God, who called Abraham out of idolatry by no merit of his own, the line of promise drawn from a house that served other gods, that all boasting be excluded. The verse displays God taking Abraham and leading him through Canaan (vv.3).
3:
"And I took your father Abraham from the other side of the flood, and led him throughout all the land of Canaan, and multiplied his seed, and gave him Isaac." The very brief verse notes God taking Abraham, leading him, and giving him Isaac. The grammatical voices note the grace. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "I took your father Abraham from the other side of the flood, and led him throughout all the land of Canaan, and multiplied his seed, and gave him Isaac" — God took Abraham from beyond the river, led him through Canaan, and gave him Isaac; the and I took your father Abraham from the other side of the flood (God took Abraham); the and led him throughout all the land of Canaan, and multiplied his seed, and gave him Isaac (led him and gave Isaac); the figure (God's free grace taking and leading Abraham, the work all God's; the truth that God took, led, multiplied, and gave, the verbs all of grace, Genesis 12:1; the figure of the sovereign grace that calls, leads, and blesses, "I took... I led... I gave"; the work of God in the patriarch; the grace of election, vv.2). The verse holds together God's grace to Abraham.

On "And I took your father Abraham from the other side of the flood, and led him throughout all the land of Canaan," the witnesses note the taking and leading. Gill notes God taking and leading Abraham (the grace, Genesis 12:1) — the taking (from the flood).

The verse displays God taking and leading Abraham and giving Isaac.

The verse displays God giving Jacob and Esau, and Esau mount Seir (vv.4).
4:
"And I gave unto Isaac Jacob and Esau: and I gave unto Esau mount Seir, to possess it; but Jacob and his children went down into Egypt." The very brief verse notes God giving Jacob and Esau, Esau Seir, and Jacob going to Egypt. The grammatical voices note the grace. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "I gave unto Isaac Jacob and Esau: and I gave unto Esau mount Seir... but Jacob and his children went down into Egypt" — God gave Isaac two sons, gave Esau Seir, and Jacob went to Egypt; the and I gave unto Isaac Jacob and Esau (Jacob and Esau given); the and I gave unto Esau mount Seir, to possess it (Esau given Seir); the but Jacob and his children went down into Egypt (Jacob to Egypt); the figure (God's choice of Jacob and the descent to Egypt, the line of promise; the truth that God gave Esau his portion but kept Jacob for the promise, the descent to Egypt the way to the nation, Genesis 46:6; the figure of the electing grace that distinguishes Jacob from Esau, "Jacob have I loved, but Esau have I hated," Romans 9:13; the choice of Jacob; the line of promise, vv.3). The verse holds together God's choice of Jacob.

5:
"I sent Moses also and Aaron, and I plagued Egypt, according to that which I did among them: and afterward I brought you out." The very brief verse notes God sending Moses and Aaron, plaguing Egypt, and bringing Israel out. The grammatical voices note the deliverance. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "I sent Moses also and Aaron, and I plagued Egypt... and afterward I brought you out" — God sent Moses and Aaron, plagued Egypt, and brought Israel out; the I sent Moses also and Aaron, and I plagued Egypt, according to that which I did among them (Moses sent, Egypt plagued); the and afterward I brought you out (brought out); the figure (God's mighty work in the deliverance from Egypt, the redemption of the nation; the truth that God sent his servants, smote Egypt, and brought Israel out, Exodus 3:10; the figure of the redemption wrought by God's power, "I brought you out"; the deliverance from Egypt; the work of God, vv.4). The verse holds together the deliverance.

On "I sent Moses also and Aaron, and I plagued Egypt, according to that which I did among them," the witnesses note the plagues. Gill notes God sending Moses and plaguing Egypt (the work, Exodus 3:10) — the plagues (Egypt smitten).

6:
"And I brought your fathers out of Egypt: and ye came unto the sea; and the Egyptians pursued after your fathers with chariots and horsemen unto the Red sea." Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "I brought your fathers out of Egypt: and ye came unto the sea; and the Egyptians pursued after your fathers with chariots and horsemen unto the Red sea" — God brought the fathers out, and the Egyptians pursued to the Red Sea; the and I brought your fathers out of Egypt: and ye came unto the sea (brought to the sea); the and the Egyptians pursued after your fathers with chariots and horsemen unto the Red sea (the pursuit); the figure (the fathers at the sea, the foe pursuing, the danger before deliverance; the truth that Israel came to the sea with the foe behind, the strait that God would open, Exodus 14:9; the figure of the danger that precedes God's deliverance, the foe pursuing; the pursuit to the Red Sea; the strait before the wonder, vv.5). The verse holds together the pursuit to the sea.

7:
"And when they cried unto the LORD, he put darkness between you and the Egyptians, and brought the sea upon them, and covered them; and your eyes have seen what I have done in Egypt: and ye dwelt in the wilderness a long season." The very brief verse notes God covering the Egyptians in the sea and keeping Israel in the wilderness. The grammatical voices note the deliverance. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "when they cried unto the LORD, he put darkness between you and the Egyptians, and brought the sea upon them, and covered them... and ye dwelt in the wilderness a long season" — God answered their cry, drowned the Egyptians, and kept Israel in the wilderness; the and when they cried unto the LORD, he put darkness between you and the Egyptians, and brought the sea upon them, and covered them (the Egyptians drowned); the and your eyes have seen what I have done in Egypt: and ye dwelt in the wilderness a long season (the wilderness years); the figure (God answering the cry, drowning the foe, and keeping the people, the deliverance and the care; the truth that God heard their cry, covered the Egyptians, and sustained them in the wilderness, Exodus 14:30; the figure of the God who delivers from the foe and sustains in the wilderness; the deliverance and the wilderness; the care of God, vv.6). The verse holds together the deliverance and the wilderness.

8:
"And I brought you into the land of the Amorites, which dwelt on the other side Jordan; and they fought with you: and I gave them into your hand, that ye might possess their land; and I destroyed them from before you." 11:

15:
"And if it seem evil unto you to serve the LORD, choose you this day whom ye will serve; whether the gods which your fathers served that were on the other side of the flood, or the gods of the Amorites, in whose land ye dwell: but as for me and my house, we will serve the LORD." The witnesses note Joshua's solemn charge to choose this day whom they would serve, and his own resolve for himself and his house — the crux of the decisive choice between God and idols, and the leader's personal commitment whatever others do. On "choose you this day whom ye will serve... but as for me and my house, we will serve the LORD," interpreters expound the choice. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the decisive choice — "and if it seem evil unto you to serve the LORD, choose you this day whom ye will serve... but as for me and my house, we will serve the LORD": Joshua sets the choice before the people and declares his own settled resolve; the witnesses note the significance — the necessity of a decisive, personal, present choice between the LORD and all idols, no neutrality possible, the service of God a deliberate act of the will (the choice set plainly, "this day," the people pressed to decide for God or against him, the heart that will not serve God will serve idols); the lesson — that every man must choose whom he will serve, there is no middle ground, and the choice must be made now, "how long halt ye between two opinions? if the LORD be God, follow him," 1 Kings 18:21; the truth that the godly leader takes his own stand whatever others do, resolving for himself and his house to serve the LORD, the head of the household leading his family in godliness, "but as for me and my house, we will serve the LORD"; the figure — the personal commitment of faith that does not wait on the multitude, the believer's resolve to serve God though all forsake him, "though all men shall be offended... yet will not I," Mark 14:29; the truth that the master of a house is bound to lead his household in the service of God, the family religion that flows from the leader's resolve, "command his children and his household after him," Genesis 18:19; the figure of the decisive choice and the leader's personal commitment; the call to choose the LORD this day and to lead one's house in his service; the warning that not to choose God is to choose idols.
